covered_sections: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable
3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables

distribution_analysis.least_covered_section: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The inequality \(|x| < 5\) is equivalent to which compound inequality?
questions.hint: Absolute value less than a positive number means the expression is between its negative and positive.
questions.explanation: The theorem states \(|k| < c\) is equivalent to \(-c < k < c\). For \(|x| < 5\), this gives \(-5 < x < 5\), which means \(x > -5\) and \(x < 5\). Option B correctly expresses this conjunction.

source_trace.content_anchor: The solutions of |k| < c are the numbers that satisfy -c < k < c

explanations_tiered.definition: The absolute value inequality \(|x| < c\) (with \(c > 0\)) means the distance from \(x\) to 0 is less than \(c\), equivalent to \(-c < x < c\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This is a conjunction ('and') inequality: \(x\) must be simultaneously greater than \(-c\) and less than \(c\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a number line: \(|x| < 5\) means x is within 5 units of zero, so x is between -5 and 5, not including -5 and 5.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(|x| < 5\) translates to \(-5 < x < 5\), which is the intersection of \(x > -5\) and \(x < 5\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The definition \(|x| = \sqrt{x^2}\) leads to \(x^2 < 25\), so \(-5 < x < 5\). This is a single interval, not two disjoint intervals.
reasoning_steps: Recall the theorem: \(|k| < c\) means \(-c < k < c\).
misconceptions: Some students incorrectly write \(|x| < 5\) as \(x < -5\) or \(x > 5\), confusing it with the 'greater than' case.

glossary_entry.definition: An inequality of the form \(|expression| < c\) where \(c > 0\), equivalent to \(-c < expression < c\).

flashcard.front: Rewrite \(|x| < 5\) as a compound inequality without absolute value.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the equivalent compound inequality for \(|x| < 5\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An inequality \(|expression| < c\) with \(c > 0\) translates to \(-c < expression < c\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize the number line interpretation: distance from zero less than c.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(|x| < 5\) means distance from 0 is less than 5, so x is between -5 and 5.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The inequality \(|2x - 1| > 7\) is equivalent to which compound inequality?
questions.hint: Absolute value greater than a positive number means the expression is outside the interval.
questions.explanation: The theorem states \(|k| > c\) is equivalent to \(k < -c\) or \(k > c\). Applying this with \(k = 2x - 1\) and \(c = 7\) gives \(2x - 1 < -7\) or \(2x - 1 > 7\). Option B correctly shows this disjunction.

source_trace.content_anchor: The solutions of |k| > c are the numbers that satisfy k > c or k < -c

explanations_tiered.definition: The absolute value inequality \(|k| > c\) (with \(c > 0\)) means the distance from \(k\) to 0 is greater than \(c\), equivalent to \(k < -c\) or \(k > c\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This is a disjunction ('or') inequality: \(k\) must be either less than \(-c\) or greater than \(c\). The solution consists of two separate intervals.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If \(|x| > 5\), x is more than 5 units away from zero, so x is either less than -5 or greater than 5.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(|2x - 1| > 7\) means \(2x - 1 < -7\) or \(2x - 1 > 7\), two separate inequalities connected by 'or'.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The definition \(|k| = \sqrt{k^2}\) gives \(k^2 > 49\), so \(k^2 - 49 > 0\), factoring as \((k-7)(k+7) > 0\), yielding \(k < -7\) or \(k > 7\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the theorem: \(|k| > c\) means \(k < -c\) or \(k > c\).
misconceptions: A common error is writing \(|k| > c\) as \(-c < k < c\), which is the rule for \(|k| < c\).

glossary_entry.definition: An inequality of the form \(|expression| > c\) with \(c > 0\), equivalent to \(expression < -c\) or \(expression > c\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you rewrite \(|2x - 1| > 7\) without absolute value?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An inequality \(|expression| > c\) translates to two separate inequalities connected by 'or'.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with the 'less than' case using number line diagrams.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(|k| > c\) means \(k < -c\) or \(k > c\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(|x + 3| < 7\). What is the solution set in interval notation?
questions.hint: Rewrite as \(-7 < x + 3 < 7\) and solve for x.
questions.explanation: \(|x + 3| < 7\) becomes \(-7 < x + 3 < 7\). Subtract 3: \(-10 < x < 4\). In interval notation, this is \((-10, 4)\). Option B would be \(|x - 3| < 7\); Options C and D represent 'greater than' cases.

explanations_tiered.definition: To solve \(|x + 3| < 7\), rewrite as a compound inequality \(-7 < x + 3 < 7\) and isolate \(x\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Subtract 3 from all three parts: \(-7 - 3 < x < 7 - 3\) gives \(-10 < x < 4\). The solution is the open interval from -10 to 4.
explanations_tiered.beginner: First, remove the absolute value by writing -7 < x+3 < 7. Then subtract 3 from all sides to get -10 < x < 4.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality \(|x + 3| < 7\) means the distance from \(x\) to \(-3\) is less than 7, so \(x\) lies in the open interval centered at \(-3\) with radius 7: \((-3-7, -3+7) = (-10, 4)\).
misconceptions: Some students forget to subtract 3 from both the left and right sides, only subtracting from one side.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: |x + a| < c → -c < x + a < c → x between -c-a and c-a.

glossary_entry.definition: An inequality of the form \(|ax + b| < c\) with \(c > 0\), solved by rewriting as \(-c < ax + b < c\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Solving Absolute Value Inequalities
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An absolute value inequality that simplifies to a linear compound inequality.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that subtracting 3 from all three parts maintains the inequality direction.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Steps for Solving Linear Absolute Value Inequalities
questions.question: Solve \(|4x - 5| \geq 3\). What is the solution set?
questions.hint: Absolute value greater than or equal to means 'or' inequality.
questions.explanation: \(|4x - 5| \geq 3\) means \(4x - 5 \leq -3\) or \(4x - 5 \geq 3\). Solving: \(4x \leq 2\) or \(4x \geq 8\), so \(x \leq \frac{1}{2}\) or \(x \geq 2\). Option B correctly shows this disjunction.

source_trace.structural_element: Steps for Solving Linear Absolute Value Inequalities
source_trace.content_anchor: If |k| >= c, then k <= -c or k >= c

explanations_tiered.definition: For \(|expression| \geq c\) with \(c > 0\), the solution is \(expression \leq -c\) or \(expression \geq c\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This produces two separate inequalities. Solve each: \(4x \leq 2\) gives \(x \leq 0.5\); \(4x \geq 8\) gives \(x \geq 2\). The solution is the union.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Split into two cases: 4x-5 ≤ -3 OR 4x-5 ≥ 3. Solve each separately, then combine with 'or'.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality \(|4x-5| \geq 3\) means the distance from \(4x-5\) to 0 is at least 3, so \(4x-5\) lies in \((-\infty, -3] \cup [3, \infty)\). Solving for x gives \((-\infty, 0.5] \cup [2, \infty)\).
reasoning_steps: Apply the 'greater than or equal' rule: \(4x - 5 \leq -3\) or \(4x - 5 \geq 3\).

formula_analysis.formula: \(|ax + b| \geq c \Rightarrow ax + b \leq -c \text{ or } ax + b \geq c\)
formula_analysis.name: Absolute value inequality (greater than or equal)

misconceptions: Some students mistakenly solve as \(-3 \leq 4x-5 \leq 3\), which is the rule for \(|k| \leq c\).

glossary_entry.term: Absolute value inequality (greater than or equal)
glossary_entry.definition: Inequality \(|expression| \geq c\) with \(c > 0\), equivalent to \(expression \leq -c\) or \(expression \geq c\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Solving Absolute Value Inequalities
publishing_metadata.glossary_term: Absolute value inequality (greater than or equal)
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An inequality where the absolute value is greater than or equal to a positive constant.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with \(|4x-5| \leq 3\) which gives a bounded interval.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The graph on a number line shows open circles at -3 and 3 with shading to the left of -3 and to the right of 3. Which absolute value inequality does this represent?
questions.hint: Open circles mean endpoints are not included. Shading outside the interval means distance greater than 3.
questions.explanation: Open circles at -3 and 3 indicate strict inequality (excluded endpoints). Shading outside (left of -3 and right of 3) means \(|x| > 3\). Options A and B shade between -3 and 3; Option D would have closed circles.

source_trace.content_anchor: |x| ≥ 1 are numbers that satisfy x < -1 or x > 1

explanations_tiered.definition: A number line with open circles and shading outside the interval between them represents \(|x| > c\) for some positive c.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Open circles at -3 and 3 mean endpoints are not included (strict inequality). Shading to the left of -3 and right of 3 indicates \(|x| > 3\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Open circles at -3 and 3 mean 'not including -3 or 3'. Arrows going outward mean 'x is farther than 3 from zero'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The shaded regions are \(x < -3\) or \(x > 3\). In absolute value form, this is \(|x| > 3\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The graph represents the solution set \(\{x \in \mathbb{R} \mid |x| > 3\}\), which is the complement of the closed interval \([-3, 3]\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the endpoints: -3 and 3, both open circles.
Identify shading: left of -3 and right of 3.
misconceptions: Students often confuse \(|x| > c\) with \(|x| < c\) when reading number line graphs.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Open circles + outward arrows = |x| > c. Closed circles + outward arrows = |x| ≥ c.

glossary_entry.definition: A number line graph showing two rays extending outward from open circles at \(-c\) and \(c\), representing \(|x| > c\).

flashcard.front: What absolute value inequality has open circles at -3 and 3 with shading outward?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What inequality is graphed with open circles at -3 and 3 and shading outward?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A number line representation of the solution set of an absolute value inequality.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Open + outward = |x| > c. Closed + outward = |x| ≥ c.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a mnemonic: 'Outward open = greater than'.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Graph absolute value inequalities on a number line
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Open circles → strict inequality; shading left and right → |x| > 3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the solution to the inequality \(|3x + 2| \leq -5\)?
questions.hint: Absolute value is always non-negative. Can it be less than or equal to a negative number?
questions.explanation: The absolute value \(|3x + 2|\) is always greater than or equal to 0. Since \(-5 < 0\), the inequality \(|3x + 2| \leq -5\) can never be true. Thus, there is no solution. Option A would be correct for \(|3x+2| \geq -5\); Option B is the solution to \(|3x+2| = 0\); Option D is incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: If absolute value is less than zero, no solution

explanations_tiered.definition: An absolute value is always non-negative, so an inequality of the form \(|expression| \leq c\) with \(c < 0\) has no solution.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since \(|3x + 2| \geq 0\) for all x, it cannot be less than or equal to \(-5\). The solution set is empty.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Absolute value can't be negative. It's impossible for a distance to be less than or equal to -5. So no x works.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(|3x + 2| \geq 0\) always. Since \(-5 < 0\), \(|3x + 2| \leq -5\) is impossible. No solution.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The range of the absolute value function is \([0, \infty)\). The inequality \(|3x+2| \leq -5\) requires the output to be in \((-\infty, -5]\), which has no intersection with \([0, \infty)\). Hence, solution set is empty.
reasoning_steps: Recall that absolute value is always ≥ 0.
Compare with right-hand side: -5 is negative.
misconceptions: Some students incorrectly solve as \(-5 \leq 3x+2 \leq 5\), forgetting that the right-hand side must be positive for the standard rule to apply.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: |expr| ≤ negative → no solution. |expr| ≥ negative → all reals.

glossary_entry.definition: An absolute value inequality where the right-hand side is negative for ≤ or <, making the inequality impossible.

flashcard.back: No solution (absolute value cannot be ≤ negative).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Absolute Value Special Cases
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No solution, because absolute value cannot be ≤ negative.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: When an absolute value inequality requires a negative value but absolute value is non-negative.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with \(|3x+2| \geq -5\) which is all real numbers.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify special cases in absolute value inequalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Absolute value is always ≥ 0, so cannot be ≤ -5.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Steps for Solving Linear Absolute Value Inequalities
questions.question: When solving \(|5x - 3| > -2\), a student concludes there is no solution. Is the student correct? Why or why not?

options.A: Yes, because the absolute value cannot be greater than a negative number
options.B: Yes, because the inequality symbol is reversed
options.C: No, because absolute value is always greater than any negative number
options.D: No, because \(-2\) should be treated as positive in the rule

questions.hint: Think about the range of absolute value. Is it ever less than a negative number?
questions.explanation: Absolute value is always \(\geq 0\). Since \(-2 < 0\), \(|5x - 3| \geq 0 > -2\) for all x. Thus, the inequality is true for all real numbers. The student is incorrect. Option D is wrong because the rule for \(|k| > c\) requires c positive for the split form, but here no split is needed.

source_trace.structural_element: Steps for Solving Linear Absolute Value Inequalities
source_trace.content_anchor: If absolute value is greater than or equal to zero, the solution is all real numbers

explanations_tiered.definition: For any real number, absolute value is non-negative. Thus, \(|expression| > c\) with \(c < 0\) is always true.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since \(|5x - 3| \geq 0\) and \(0 > -2\), the inequality \(|5x - 3| > -2\) holds for every real x. The solution is all real numbers, not no solution.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Absolute value is always zero or positive. Any positive or zero number is always greater than -2. So every x works.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(|5x - 3| \geq 0 > -2\) for all x. Therefore, the inequality is true for all real numbers.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The range of \(f(x) = |5x-3|\) is \([0, \infty)\). Since this entire range is contained in \((-2, \infty)\), the solution set is all \(\mathbb{R}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that absolute value is always ≥ 0.
Conclusion: all real numbers satisfy the inequality.
misconceptions: Students incorrectly apply the split rule |k| > c → k < -c or k > c even when c is negative, leading to errors.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: |expr| > negative → all real numbers. |expr| < negative → no solution.

glossary_entry.definition: When an absolute value inequality has a negative right-hand side for > or ≥, the solution is all real numbers.

flashcard.back: Yes. Absolute value ≥ 0 > -2, so always true.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Absolute Value Special Cases
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: True or false: \(|5x-3| > -2\) has no solution.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: False. Absolute value ≥ 0 > -2, so all real numbers are solutions.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: When the right-hand side of an absolute value inequality with > or ≥ is negative, the solution is all real numbers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the split rule only applies when c ≥ 0. For c < 0, reasoning about range is needed.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Recognize special solution sets in absolute value inequalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The student is incorrect; absolute value is always > -2 for all x.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A student solves \(|2x - 1| < 5\) and writes the solution as \(x < 3\) or \(x > -2\). What error did the student make?

options.C: The student should have used closed circles on the number line

questions.hint: Remember the difference between 'less than' and 'greater than' cases for absolute value.
questions.explanation: For \(|k| < c\), the equivalent is \(-c < k < c\), which is an 'and' inequality (conjunction). The student wrote \(x < 3\) or \(x > -2\), which is a disjunction. The correct solution is \(x > -2\) and \(x < 3\), i.e., \(-2 < x < 3\).

source_trace.content_anchor: |k| < c are numbers that satisfy -c < k < c

explanations_tiered.definition: A common misconception is confusing the conjunction ('and') for \(|k| < c\) with the disjunction ('or') for \(|k| > c\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Correct solution: \(-5 < 2x-1 < 5\) → add 1: \(-4 < 2x < 6\) → divide by 2: \(-2 < x < 3\). This is \(x > -2\) and \(x < 3\), not 'or'.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The student used the rule for 'greater than' on a 'less than' problem. 'Less than' means 'between', which uses 'and'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(|2x-1| < 5\) is a 'between' inequality, giving a single continuous interval. 'Or' would give two separate intervals. Correct: \(-2 < x < 3\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The correct form is a conjunction because the absolute value inequality restricts \(2x-1\) to a bounded interval around 0, resulting in a single interval for x.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that |k| < c is a 'between' inequality.
Solve the compound inequality using 'and' logic.
The solution is a single interval, not two separate intervals.
misconceptions: Students frequently memorize 'or' for absolute value and incorrectly apply it to both < and > cases.

glossary_entry.definition: Mistaking the logical connector in absolute value inequalities, using 'or' when 'and' is required or vice versa.

flashcard.front: What is the common error when solving \(|2x-1| < 5\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What mistake did the student make solving \(|2x-1| < 5\) as \(x < 3\) or \(x > -2\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Confusing the logical connector in absolute value inequality solutions.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use number line diagrams to show that 'and' gives overlap, 'or' gives union.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Avoid common errors in solving absolute value inequalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The student used 'or' (disjunction) instead of 'and' (conjunction), which is the rule for |k| > c.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The interval \((-6, 2)\) on a number line has open circles at -6 and 2 with shading between them. Which absolute value inequality matches this graph?
questions.hint: Find the midpoint of the interval; that's the shift inside the absolute value. The distance from the midpoint to an endpoint is the constant.
questions.explanation: The interval \((-6, 2)\) has midpoint \(\frac{-6+2}{2} = -2\). The distance from -2 to either endpoint is 4. Thus, the inequality is \(|x - (-2)| = |x + 2| < 4\). Option A is correct. Option B would give midpoint 2; Options C and D are 'greater than' cases.

explanations_tiered.definition: An absolute value inequality of the form \(|x - h| < r\) represents the open interval \((h-r, h+r)\) on the number line.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Midpoint = center \(h\), radius = distance from center to endpoint. For \((-6, 2)\), \(h = -2\), \(r = 4\). Thus, \(|x - (-2)| = |x + 2| < 4\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find the middle of -6 and 2, which is -2. The distance from -2 to 2 is 4. So x is within 4 of -2: \(|x - (-2)| < 4\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The interval \((-6, 2)\) can be written as \(\{x : -6 < x < 2\}\). This equals \(\{x : |x + 2| < 4\}\) because the center is -2 and radius is 4.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For any open interval \((a,b)\), the absolute value inequality is \(|x - \frac{a+b}{2}| < \frac{b-a}{2}\). Here, \(\frac{-6+2}{2} = -2\), \(\frac{2-(-6)}{2} = 4\), so \(|x + 2| < 4\).
misconceptions: Students often incorrectly use \(|x - 2| < 4\) for interval (-6,2), confusing the sign of the center.

glossary_entry.definition: Converting an open interval (a,b) to the absolute value inequality |x - (a+b)/2| < (b-a)/2.

flashcard.front: What absolute value inequality represents the interval \((-6, 2)\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Interval to Absolute Value
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Convert \((-6, 2)\) to an absolute value inequality.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Finding the center and radius of an interval to write as |x - h| < r.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to distance on a number line: distance from x to -2 is less than 4.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Translate between interval notation and absolute value inequalities

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which condition must be met for the absolute value inequality \(|ax + b| \leq c\) to have exactly one solution?
questions.hint: When does an absolute value inequality become an equation with a single solution?
questions.explanation: If \(c = 0\), the inequality \(|ax + b| \leq 0\) forces \(|ax + b| = 0\), which gives exactly one solution: \(ax + b = 0\). If \(c < 0\), no solution; if \(c > 0\), infinitely many solutions. Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: If absolute value is less than or equal to zero, there is one solution

explanations_tiered.definition: The inequality \(|ax+b| \leq 0\) has exactly one solution because absolute value is non-negative, so it forces the expression inside to be zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If \(c = 0\), \(|ax+b| \leq 0\) implies \(|ax+b| = 0\), so \(ax+b = 0\). This linear equation has exactly one solution (provided \(a \neq 0\)).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If c = 0, the only way an absolute value can be ≤ 0 is if it equals 0. That gives one solution.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(c = 0\): \(|ax+b| \leq 0\) means \(|ax+b| = 0\) ⇒ \(ax+b = 0\) ⇒ one solution. For \(c < 0\): no solution. For \(c > 0\): infinitely many solutions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution set of \(|ax+b| \leq c\) is \([-c, c]\) shifted and scaled. Its cardinality is infinite for \(c > 0\), exactly one for \(c = 0\) (the single point where \(ax+b = 0\)), and empty for \(c < 0\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that |expression| ≥ 0 for all real numbers.
If c < 0, |expr| ≤ c is impossible (no solution).
If c = 0, |expr| ≤ 0 forces |expr| = 0, giving expr = 0 (one solution if a ≠ 0).
If c > 0, there are infinitely many solutions forming an interval.
misconceptions: Students often think |ax+b| ≤ 0 has no solution, forgetting that absolute value can equal zero.

glossary_entry.definition: When the right-hand side of an absolute value inequality with ≤ or ≥ is zero, the solution is a single point.

flashcard.front: When does \(|ax+b| \leq c\) have exactly one solution?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Absolute Value Special Cases
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When does \(|ax+b| \leq c\) have exactly one solution?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The special case of an absolute value inequality with ≤ or ≥ and right-hand side zero, reducing to an equation with one solution.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify special solution sets in absolute value inequalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. When c = 0, |ax+b| ≤ 0 forces |ax+b| = 0, giving exactly one solution.
